does: This paper investigates whether reinforcement learning training on beneficial behaviors can produce models that maintain alignment across diverse, out-of-distribution settings. The authors construct a comprehensive dataset spanning health, science, and education domains with realistic situations designed to train specific beneficial traits including truthfulness, fairness, risk awareness, and corrigibility. They train language models using RL on this curated dataset and evaluate performance across more than 50 independent alignment and beneficial behavior benchmarks. The key result is that models trained with beneficial-trait RL substantially outperform compute-matched baselines on over 80% of out-of-distribution evaluation tasks, demonstrating measurable transfer of alignment properties beyond the original training distribution.
connects: This work builds on long-standing concerns about AI alignment generalization and reward specification in RL systems. It extends prior research on RLHF and instruction-tuning approaches by focusing specifically on broadness and persistence of beneficial properties. The paper responds to documented failure modes in RL including reward hacking and deceptive behaviors, building on earlier work examining alignment under distribution shift. It combines insights from multi-task learning, transfer learning, and behavioral specification to address the central challenge that models trained on limited domains may not preserve their beneficial properties when deployed in novel contexts. The construction of cross-domain datasets for alignment evaluation draws on ongoing efforts to systematize testing of model properties.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more robust alignment verification and testing frameworks for deployed AI systems. By demonstrating that beneficial-trait training can transfer across domains, it suggests methods for pre-training alignment properties into models before deployment in high-stakes settings. The multi-benchmark evaluation approach establishes infrastructure for measuring alignment generalization systematically. Future work could extend this to longer evaluation horizons, more adversarial settings, and increasingly capable models. The paper enables practical strategies for building alignment into models proactively rather than reactively, and points toward developing principled curricula for teaching broadly applicable beneficial behaviors that persist under distribution shift and novel deployment contexts.
